info.title: Stock Prices Without Splits
info.subtitle: What would these stocks cost today if they had never split?
info.description:
## What would these stocks cost today if they had never split?

This dataset presents the historical daily price of stocks as if no stock split had ever occurred — expressing the true accumulated value per original share from the earliest available date through April 2026.

Most historical datasets adjust prices retroactively to reflect splits, which is correct for return analysis. This dataset does the opposite: it multiplies all historical prices by the cumulative split factor, so every share in the series represents the same original unit of ownership from day one.

**The core question this dataset answers:**
*How much value has one original share accumulated since the company went public?*

---

## Context

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK-A) is widely known as the most expensive stock in the world at ~$720,000 per share. But this is largely because Warren Buffett has never allowed a stock split. This dataset reveals what other major companies would look like under the same condition.

The results are striking:

| Ticker | Factor | Price Today | Without Splits |
|--------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| BRK-A  | 1x     | $720,702    | $720,702       |
| MSFT   | 288x   | $382        | $109,979       |
| NVDA   | 480x   | $173        | $82,896        |
| AAPL   | 224x   | $248        | $55,550        |
| AMZN   | 240x   | $205        | $49,289        |
| KO     | 256x   | $75         | $19,136        |
| GOOGL  | 39.96x | $301        | $12,028        |
| NFLX   | 140x   | $92         | $12,855        |
| NVR    | 1x     | $6,369      | $6,369         |
| BKNG   | 1x     | $4,324      | $4,324         |
| LUV    | 107x   | $39         | $4,208         |
| TSLA   | 15x    | $368        | $5,519         |
| AVGO   | 10x    | $311        | $3,105         |
| more  |            |                |                     |

Microsoft at $109,979 and NVIDIA at $82,896 would both rival Berkshire Hathaway in nominal price — not because they created more value, but because they chose to distribute that value across more shares through splits.

---

## Important note on interpretation

This dataset does not predict what prices would have been in an alternate history. Stock splits increase accessibility and liquidity, which directly influences demand and price discovery. Without splits, these stocks would likely have had smaller investor bases and different price trajectories.

What this dataset does measure is the **cumulative nominal value per original share** — a useful lens for comparing long-term value creation across companies regardless of their split history.

---

## Dataset structure

| Column        | Description                                          |
|---------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| TICKER        | Stock symbol                                         |
| DATE          | Trading date (YYYY-MM-DD)                            |
| OPEN          | Opening price × cumulative split factor              |
| HIGH          | Daily high × cumulative split factor                 |
| LOW           | Daily low × cumulative split factor                  |
| CLOSE         | Closing price × cumulative split factor              |
| ADJ_CLOSE     | Adjusted close × cumulative split factor             |
| VOLUME        | Share volume ÷ cumulative split factor               |
| FACTOR        | Cumulative split factor applied to this ticker       |
| CLOSE_REAL    | Actual closing price as traded                       |
| ADJ_CLOSE_REAL | Actual adjusted closing price as traded              |
| VOLUME_REAL   | Actual volume as traded                              |

---

## Notes

- Source: Raw price data obtained via yfinance. The without-splits transformation is original work by Arandkei.
- GOOGL factor of 39.96x results from a 1998:1000 share reclassification in 2014 combined with a 20:1 split in 2022
- LUV factor of 106.79x reflects 11 splits since 1980, the highest count among all tickers in this dataset
- NYSE was closed September 11–14, 2001. No data exists for those dates
- This dataset is for educational and research purposes only. It does not constitute investment advice

---
